title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim) Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make delicious Spicy Korean Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim) with this easy vegetarian recipe. Packed with flavor, it's perfect for a healthy and satisfying meal. Get the best recipe here!
longTailKeywords: how to make spicy Korean braised tofu
best spicy Korean tofu recipe at home
seo.h1: Authentic Dubu Jorim: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe
h2Headings: What is Dubu Jorim (Spicy Korean Braised Tofu)?
Ingredients for Spicy Korean Braised Tofu
How to Make Dubu Jorim (Step-by-Step Instructions)
Tips for the Best Spicy Braised Tofu
Frequently Asked Questions about Dubu Jorim
seo.imageAlt: A bowl of spicy Korean braised tofu (Dubu Jorim) garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich and spicy flavors of homemade Dubu Jorim, a classic Korean vegetarian dish.

description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean vegetarian dish featuring pan-fried tofu simmered in a rich, savory, and spicy gochujang-based sauce. This comforting and flavorful meal highlights the versatility of tofu, absorbing all the delicious spices. It is a perfect side dish or main course, offering a satisfying kick with every bite.

instructions.title: Prepare and Fry the Tofu
instructions.text: Drain the tofu block thoroughly and press it for at least 30 minutes to remove excess water. Slice the pressed tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles. Heat vegetable oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Pan-fry the tofu slices for 3-4 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove from pan and set aside.

instructions.title: Prepare the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: While the tofu fries, combine gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, minced garlic, minced ginger (if using), and sesame oil in a medium bowl. Whisk until all ingredients are well combined and form a smooth paste. This is your flavorful braising sauce.

instructions.title: Combine Tofu and Sauce
instructions.text: Carefully arrange the pan-fried tofu slices back into the same pan or a shallow pot in a single layer. Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Ensure each piece is coated.

instructions.title: Add Liquid and Simmer
instructions.text: Pour the water or vegetable broth into the pan with the tofu and sauce. Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise.

instructions.title: Braise and Thicken
instructions.text: Continue to simmer for 15-20 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened and the tofu has absorbed the rich flavors. Periodically, gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even coating. The sauce should reduce to a glossy, flavorful glaze.

instructions.title: Garnish and Serve
instructions.text: Remove the Dubu Jorim from heat. Garnish generously with freshly chopped green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with warm rice and other Korean side dishes (banchan).

tips: Always press your tofu thoroughly to achieve a firm texture that absorbs sauce beautifully and prevents it from crumbling during cooking.
Adjust the amount of gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) to control the spice level. For a milder dish, reduce it; for extra heat, add more.
Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the flavors meld. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days. It pairs perfectly with freshly cooked rice.
article:
Dubu Jorim, or Spicy Braised Tofu, is a beloved staple in Korean cuisine that beautifully showcases how humble ingredients can be transformed into a dish bursting with flavor and character. Often overshadowed by meat-centric Korean dishes, this vegetarian gem proves that plant-based options can be just as satisfying, if not more so. If you're looking for a healthy, delicious, and incredibly versatile dish that packs a punch, then Dubu Jorim is your answer. It's a fantastic way to introduce your palate to the vibrant, spicy, and savory notes that define Korean cooking, all while enjoying the wholesome goodness of tofu. Whether you're a seasoned Korean food enthusiast or just beginning your culinary exploration, learning to make this delightful Spicy Korean Braised Tofu at home is a rewarding experience that promises a flavorful meal every time.

## What is Dubu Jorim (Spicy Korean Braised Tofu)?

Dubu Jorim literally translates to "tofu braised in sauce." "Dubu" means tofu, and "Jorim" refers to a cooking technique where ingredients are simmered in a seasoned liquid until the sauce is reduced and thickened, coating the main ingredient. At its core, Dubu Jorim involves pan-frying slices of firm tofu until golden brown, then simmering them in a rich, savory, and spicy sauce made primarily from gochujang (Korean chili paste) and gochugaru (Korean chili powder), along with soy sauce, garlic, and other aromatics.

This dish is a testament to the simplicity and depth of Korean vegetarian cooking. It's commonly served as a "banchan" (side dish) alongside rice and other Korean dishes, but it's substantial enough to be enjoyed as a light main course. The magic of Dubu Jorim lies in the contrast of textures – the slightly crispy exterior of the pan-fried tofu giving way to a soft interior, all enveloped in a sticky, flavorful, and wonderfully spicy sauce. It's a comforting, wholesome, and incredibly addictive example of **Spicy Korean Braised Tofu**.

## Ingredients for Spicy Korean Braised Tofu

To create the perfect Dubu Jorim, you’ll need a few key ingredients, most of which are readily available in Asian supermarkets or even well-stocked regular grocery stores.

*   **For the Tofu:**
    *   **1 block (14-16 oz) extra-firm or firm tofu:** This is crucial. Softer tofu will fall apart during frying and braising. Pressing the tofu is essential for a good texture and for it to absorb the sauce better.
    *   **Oil for frying:** Any neutral oil like vegetable, canola, or grapeseed oil will work.

*   **For the Jorim Sauce:**
    *   **2 tablespoons soy sauce:** Provides the foundational savory, umami flavor.
    *   **1-2 tablespoons gochujang (Korean chili paste):** Adds depth, sweetness, and a lovely fermented tang, alongside its heat. Adjust to your spice preference.
    *   **1 tablespoon gochugaru (Korean chili powder):** Primarily for heat and vibrant color. Use less if you prefer milder spice.
    *   **1 tablespoon sugar or corn syrup:** Balances the spice and salt, adding a touch of sweetness.
    *   **1 tablespoon minced garlic:** A cornerstone of Korean flavor.
    *   **1 teaspoon minced ginger (optional but recommended):** Adds a fresh, pungent kick.
    *   **1 tablespoon sesame oil:** For a nutty aroma and flavor at the end.
    *   **1/4 cup water or vegetable broth:** To create the braising liquid.
    *   **1-2 green onions (scallions):** Sliced, for garnish and a fresh, mild oniony flavor.
    *   **Toasted sesame seeds (optional):** For garnish.

These ingredients combine to create the iconic flavor profile that makes **Spicy Korean Braised Tofu** so irresistible.

## How to Make Dubu Jorim (Step-by-Step Instructions)

Making Dubu Jorim is a straightforward process that yields incredibly delicious results. Follow these steps for a perfect batch:

1.  **Prepare the Tofu:** Remove the tofu from its packaging and drain any excess water. Wrap the block in a few layers of paper towels or a clean kitchen towel. Place a heavy object (like a cutting board with cans on top) on the tofu for at least 30 minutes, or up to an hour, to press out as much water as possible. This step is vital for firm texture and better sauce absorption. Once pressed, slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles or squares.
2.  **Pan-Fry the Tofu:** Heat 2-3 tablespoons of oil in a large non-stick skillet over medium-high heat. Once hot, carefully add the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-5 minutes per side, or until golden brown and slightly crispy. This creates a wonderful texture and helps the tofu hold its shape during braising. Remove the fried tofu from the pan and set aside on a plate lined with paper towels to absorb any excess oil.
3.  **Prepare the Sauce:** While the tofu fries or after it's done, whisk together all the sauce ingredients in a small bowl: soy sauce, gochujang, gochugaru, sugar (or corn syrup), minced garlic, minced ginger (if using), sesame oil, and water (or vegetable broth). Stir until well combined and the sugar has dissolved.
4.  **Braise the Tofu:** In the same skillet (you can wipe it clean if there's too much residue, but a little browning is fine), pour in the prepared sauce. Bring it to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Carefully add the fried tofu slices back into the pan, arranging them in a single layer. Spoon some of the sauce over each piece of tofu to ensure even coating.
5.  **Simmer and Reduce:** Reduce the heat to medium-low, cover the pan, and let the tofu simmer in the sauce for 5-7 minutes. Then, uncover the pan and continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened and reduced to your desired consistency, coating the tofu beautifully.
6.  **Garnish and Serve:** Remove from heat. Garnish generously with sliced green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds (if using). Serve immediately with warm rice.

## Tips for the Best Spicy Braised Tofu

*   **Press Your Tofu Thoroughly:** This cannot be stressed enough. Well-pressed tofu has a firmer texture, fries better, and absorbs the flavorful sauce like a sponge.
*   **Don't Skimp on Frying:** Achieving a nice golden crust on your tofu before braising adds a fantastic textural contrast and prevents the tofu from becoming mushy.
*   **Adjust Spice to Your Liking:** The amount of gochujang and gochugaru listed provides a medium level of spice. If you prefer it milder, reduce the gochugaru or omit it entirely. For more heat, add an extra dash of gochugaru.
*   **Let It Rest (Optional):** Like many braised dishes, the flavors of Dubu Jorim often deepen and meld beautifully if allowed to sit for 15-30 minutes after cooking, or even overnight in the fridge.
*   **Use Quality Ingredients:** Fresh garlic and ginger make a noticeable difference in the overall flavor profile. Good quality gochujang and gochugaru are also key.

## Serving Suggestions for Dubu Jorim

Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in many ways. It shines brightest when served alongside freshly steamed white or brown rice, allowing the rice to soak up every last drop of the delicious, spicy sauce.

*   **As a Banchan:** Its most traditional role is as a savory side dish in a larger Korean meal, complementing other banchan like kimchi, various namul (seasoned vegetables), and a main protein.
*   **As a Main Dish:** For a light yet satisfying vegetarian meal, serve a generous portion of Dubu Jorim with rice and perhaps a simple green salad.
*   **With Noodles:** It can also be a wonderful topping for a bowl of plain ramen or udon noodles.
*   **In a Bibimbap Bowl:** Cut the braised tofu into smaller pieces and add it to a bibimbap bowl with rice, sautéed vegetables, and a fried egg (if not strictly vegan).
*   **Wrapped in Lettuce:** For a fresh take, serve it with ssam (lettuce wraps), adding a piece of tofu and a dollop of rice to a lettuce leaf for a refreshing bite.

## Variations & Substitutions

Dubu Jorim is highly adaptable, allowing you to customize it to your preferences or dietary needs.

*   **Make it Less Spicy:** Reduce the amount of gochujang and gochugaru, or replace some of the gochugaru with sweet paprika for color without the heat.
*   **Add Vegetables:** Enhance the dish by braising other quick-cooking vegetables alongside the tofu. Sliced onions, zucchini, mushrooms, bell peppers, or even leafy greens like spinach or napa cabbage can be added during the simmering stage.
*   **Gluten-Free Option:** Simply substitute regular soy sauce with tamari for a gluten-free version of this **Spicy Korean Braised Tofu**.
*   **Sweeter Sauce:** If you prefer a slightly sweeter profile, increase the amount of sugar or corn syrup in the sauce. A touch of mirin (rice wine) can also add sweetness and depth.
*   **Different Tofu Texture:** While extra-firm is recommended for its sturdiness, you can use firm tofu if that's all you have. Just handle it more gently. Silken tofu is generally too delicate for this preparation.

## Frequently Asked Questions about Dubu Jorim

**Q: Can I make Dubu Jorim ahead of time?**
A: Absolutely! Dubu Jorim is one of those dishes that often tastes even better the next day as the flavors have more time to meld and deepen. Store it in an airtight container in the refrigerator.

**Q: How do I store leftovers?**
A: Store any leftover Dubu Jorim in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days. Reheat gently on the stovetop or in the microwave.

**Q: Is Dubu Jorim vegan?**
A: Yes, if you use vegetable broth instead of water (and ensure your gochujang doesn't contain any non-vegan ingredients, though most are vegan-friendly), this dish is entirely vegan.

**Q: What kind of tofu is best for this recipe?**
A: Extra-firm or firm tofu is highly recommended. These types hold their shape well during frying and braising, preventing the tofu from crumbling.

**Q: How spicy is Dubu Jorim typically?**
A: The traditional recipe has a medium level of spice, offering a pleasant kick without being overwhelmingly hot. However, the spice level is easily adjustable by modifying the amounts of gochujang and gochugaru.

Dubu Jorim is a truly satisfying and flavorful dish that exemplifies the best of Korean vegetarian cuisine. Its vibrant flavors, comforting warmth, and wholesome ingredients make it a perfect addition to any meal. Don't hesitate to try making this delightful **Spicy Korean Braised Tofu** at home – your taste buds will thank you!
